title: OpenAI just SOLVED MATH....
summary: Wes Roth discusses OpenAI's internal model breakthrough in mathematical reasoning, covering the technical approach ("secret sauce") and downstream implications. The video frames this as a significant advancement in how AI systems handle math problems.
writeup: An unreleased OpenAI general reasoning model disproved the Erdős planar unit distance conjecture, posed in 1946, by finding an infinite family of point arrangements that surpass the grid layout for maximizing 1-unit-distance connections. The method was striking: the model constructed a higher-dimensional lattice and projected it down to a 2D shadow, analogous to a shadow-sculpture whose jumbled form casts a recognizable silhouette. Harvard mathematician Melanie Matchett Wood verified the result and confirmed it as a genuine, publishable discovery on a prominent open problem. The breakthrough carries an unsettling implication she articulated: the same experts who checked the proof could likely have found the same counterexample themselves, in the same amount of time, had they simply been assembled to look.
key_points: OpenAI's unreleased internal reasoning model disproved the Erdős planar unit distance conjecture, a problem posed in 1946 that asks how quickly the number of 1-unit-distance connections between n points can grow as n scales to infinity.
The model found an infinite family of point arrangements that beat the grid layout by a meaningful margin, directly contradicting Erdős's belief that grid-like configurations were optimal.

The solution method involved constructing a higher-dimensional lattice and mapping it down to a 2D projection, a technique compared to a shadow-sculpture: an unrecognizable 3D structure whose shadow resolves into a clear image.

Harvard mathematician Melanie Matchett Wood verified the proof, confirmed it as a novel discovery, and noted that all the human experts assembled to check the result could have found the same counterexample themselves in the same time had they been tasked with finding one.

This result stands in contrast to an OpenAI claim seven months earlier that was widely criticized as incorrect, with Demis Hassabis calling it embarrassing; this time, mathematicians are fully on board with the result.

The video frames the key question not as whether the AI invented new mathematics, but why the assembled human expertise did not converge on this solution independently when all the pieces were already known.
